facts_disclosed: Sarah wants to move to Bend

turns.content: I need to think through something that has been occupying a considerable amount of mental real estate lately. My wife Sarah has been making a sustained case for us to move to Bend.

That's a big conversation. Bend is a beautiful area, but moving to a new city touches everything -- career, community, daily life. It sounds like this isn't a passing comment from Sarah but something she's been seriously advocating for. What's driving it from her perspective?

She has a whole portfolio of arguments. Better access to hiking and outdoor recreation, which is fair -- the trails around Bend are extraordinary. More space for the money. Lower cost of living compared to Portland. A quieter pace of life. She has been researching it methodically, which is how I know she is serious. Sarah does not bring things up casually.

When someone builds a researched case rather than just floating an idea, they've already moved past the daydreaming phase. And her arguments aren't wrong on the merits -- Bend does offer all of those things. The outdoor access is genuinely world-class, and the housing market is still more manageable than Portland.

But I'm guessing you have a different set of considerations. What's your side of this?

My side is more complicated and less tidy, which is probably why she is winning the argument on points. The practical reality is that I am a high school teacher with twenty-four years at my current school. I have seniority, I have relationships with colleagues and students, I have a reputation I have built over decades. Moving to Bend means starting over at a new school where no one knows me. At fifty-two, the prospect of being the new teacher walking into a faculty meeting is not exactly thrilling.

That's not a trivial concern -- it's foundational. Twenty-four years at one school isn't just a line on a resume. It's institutional knowledge, trust with administration, a network of former students, the ability to teach the way you want because you've earned that autonomy. Starting over means rebuilding all of that from scratch, and at fifty-two, the timeline for getting back to that level of professional comfort is real.

Have you and Sarah talked about the career dimension specifically, or has the conversation stayed mostly on the lifestyle side?

We have talked about it. Her position is that good teachers find jobs, which is true in the abstract but ignores the specifics. Bend is not a large city. The number of high school teaching positions available in any given year is limited, and finding one that matches my subject area and experience level is not guaranteed. Sarah is a librarian, so for her, library positions exist in every county system. She acknowledges the Deschutes County system is smaller, but she frames that as a trade-off she is willing to make. The asymmetry is that her career transplants more easily than mine does.

That's the core asymmetry, and it's a real one. She's willing to accept a professional downgrade for the lifestyle upgrade, and she's asking you to make a similar calculation. But the difference is that your professional identity seems more tightly bound to your specific role and institution. Teaching AP-level courses at a school that values what you bring requires a specific opening at a specific kind of school. In a smaller market, those openings happen on someone else's timeline, not yours.

Has Sarah looked into the school situation in Bend at all, or is that part of the conversation still abstract?

She has, actually. She pulled up the Bend-La Pine school district website and showed me that they have three high schools, including one that opened relatively recently. She pointed out that a growing district means openings. I conceded the logic but noted that an opening in social studies at a school that offers AP History is a much narrower target than just any teaching position. She filed that under problems to solve later, which is Sarah's way of acknowledging a point without letting it derail her argument.

That's a very Sarah-like move from what you've described -- she acknowledges the complexity without giving it veto power over the idea. And she's not entirely wrong that a growing district creates more opportunities than a stable one. But you're right that AP History is specialized enough that "openings exist" and "the right opening exists" are very different statements.

Is there a part of you that finds the idea appealing at all, or is this genuinely one-sided?

I would be lying if I said the hiking did not tempt me. Bend sits at the doorstep of some of the most beautiful landscape in the state. And Portland has changed in ways that have been hard to watch -- the city I moved to twenty-five years ago is not the city it is today. There are days when I am sympathetic to the argument. But then I think about walking into a school where I have no history, no context, no relationships, and the appeal evaporates rather quickly.

That tension is the crux of it. The lifestyle pull is real and you can feel it, but the professional roots you've put down are deep. And the Portland observation is telling -- if you felt completely anchored to the city itself, the conversation would be simpler. The fact that Portland's changes have loosened some of that attachment means Sarah's argument has more purchase than it might have five years ago.

What about the social side beyond work? Do you have a community in Portland that would be hard to leave, or is most of your network connected to the school?

That is a perceptive question, and the answer is somewhat uncomfortable. Most of my social life is tied to the school. My closest friends are colleagues. Our social calendar revolves around school events, the occasional dinner with other teachers and their spouses, and the hiking that Sarah and I do together. We are not deeply embedded in a Portland community outside of my work. Sarah has pointed this out, gently but effectively. She argues that we could build a social life in Bend just as easily because most of our socializing is not location-dependent -- it is activity-dependent.

That's an argument that's hard to counter. If your social life is mostly work-adjacent and activity-based, then a move to Bend doesn't destroy a rich community network -- it replaces one set of colleagues with another, and it significantly upgrades the activity side. Sarah's framing is strategic: she's not asking you to give up a vibrant Portland social scene because, as you've described it, that's not really what you have.

Have you two discussed what the actual housing situation would look like? The cost comparison between Portland and Bend in concrete terms?

She has spreadsheets. I am not exaggerating. Sarah pulled comparable homes in both markets -- what our Portland house would sell for, what we could buy in Bend for the same money or less. The numbers are genuinely compelling. We could get a larger house on more land with a lower mortgage, or potentially no mortgage at all depending on how things shake out. She also factored in property tax differences. It is a thorough document. I told her she missed her calling as a prosecutor.

A librarian with spreadsheets and comparable market analyses is a formidable negotiating opponent. And the financial argument for Bend over Portland is legitimate -- you'd likely gain square footage and land while reducing or eliminating a mortgage, which has cascading effects on financial flexibility, especially approaching retirement years.

The prosecutor comparison seems apt. It sounds like she's built a case on every front: lifestyle, finances, housing, even your social patterns. What's left on your side of the ledger that she hasn't addressed?

Identity, I think. And I realize that sounds dramatic, but after twenty-four years, my sense of who I am is deeply intertwined with being a teacher at this specific school. I know the building, I know which classroom has the best afternoon light, I know which custodian will lend me a dolly when I need to move textbooks. Those sound like trivial details, but they add up to a feeling of belonging that I am not sure I can replicate. Starting over is not just a logistical challenge. It is an existential one.

That doesn't sound dramatic at all -- it sounds honest. There's a difference between the tangible things you'd lose and the intangible sense of place that comes from decades of accumulated familiarity. The classroom with the right light, the custodian with the dolly -- those aren't trivial. They're the texture of a professional life that can't be transferred.

But here's the thing worth sitting with: that sense of belonging was built. You didn't have it on day one at Lincoln. You built it over twenty-four years. The question isn't whether you could build it again somewhere else -- you almost certainly could. The question is whether you want to, and whether the cost of rebuilding is worth what you'd gain.

That is the question, and I do not have an answer yet. Sarah is patient, but she is also persistent. She has not issued an ultimatum, and she would not -- that is not how we operate. But I can feel the weight of her wanting this, and I do not want to be the person who stands in the way of something that would make her genuinely happy. The guilt of that is its own pressure.

That guilt is worth examining carefully, because it can lead to a decision made for the wrong reasons. Saying yes out of guilt is different from saying yes because you've genuinely come around. And Sarah, from everything you've described, would not want a reluctant yes -- she'd want a real one.

Have you two discussed a timeline? Is she talking about this as a "next year" plan or something further out?

She would move tomorrow if it were up to her. Realistically, she is talking about the next two to three years. She is smart enough to know that if she lets me defer it indefinitely, it will never happen. And I do not think it is the wrong move for Sarah -- I am simply not certain it is the right move for me. We are not disagreeing about facts. We are weighing different values. She prioritizes the life we would live outside of work. I am more attached to the life I have built inside of work. Neither of us is wrong, which means there is no clean resolution.

That's a remarkably clear-eyed articulation of the problem. It's a genuine conflict between two legitimate sets of values, and the resolution requires one of you to sacrifice something real -- or both of you to find a compromise that partially satisfies both.

One thing that might help: could you name a condition that would make you willing? Something like, "If I can find an AP position at a school I respect, I'll do it." That shifts you from a blanket no to a conditional yes, which gives both of you something to work toward. It protects the thing you value most -- your professional identity -- while leaving the door open to the life Sarah is envisioning.

The conditional yes is an interesting framework. It would give me a concrete standard to evaluate rather than an amorphous dread of change. And it would show Sarah that I am taking her seriously rather than just stalling. At minimum, it moves us from a stalemate to a process, which is progress of a kind.

Moving from a stalemate to a process is exactly right. Sarah gets forward motion and a genuine engagement with the idea, and you get a standard that protects what matters to you. It doesn't guarantee a particular outcome, but it gives the conversation somewhere productive to go.

I appreciate you letting me think through this without pushing me in either direction. It is the kind of decision that does not benefit from advice so much as from articulation. Sometimes you need to hear yourself say the thing out loud before you understand what you actually think. I still do not know what we will do. But I have a better sense of why it is difficult, which is its own kind of progress.

That's a very generous way to describe what happened here -- you did the thinking, I just gave you a surface to think against. And you're right that some decisions don't resolve through analysis. They resolve through lived time and the slow accumulation of clarity.

Good luck with the conversation. It sounds like you and Sarah are both approaching this with honesty and care, which is more than most couples manage with decisions this big.
